title: Insight Illusion
en_def: The perception of meaningful pattern discovery from AI-generated visualizations where the pattern exists primarily in the interpretation framework rather than in underlying data distributions.

title: Overfitting Confidence
en_def: The state where a data practitioner expresses high certainty about model performance on unseen data following automatic feature selection by an AI system, despite the model's complexity exceeding the information content of the training set.

title: Preprocessing Trust Collapse
en_def: The moment when a data professional realizes their confidence in algorithmic data cleaning exceeded validation of the cleaning outcomes against domain knowledge.

title: Dashboard Refresh Loops
en_def: Repetitive checking behavior directed at AI-generated dashboards, characterized by high refresh frequency that exceeds the underlying data update cadence.

title: Data Cleanliness Assumption
en_def: The inference that data processed through AI-driven cleaning pipelines contains fewer errors, inconsistencies, or anomalies than pre-processed data, without comparative empirical evidence.

title: Feature Engineering Delegation
en_def: The transfer of feature creation and selection decisions from human domain experts to automated machine learning systems, resulting in feature sets whose construction logic remains unexamined.

title: Domain Expertise Displacement
en_def: The reduction in direct application of domain-specific knowledge as AI systems handle data interpretation, modeling, and insight generation tasks previously requiring expert judgment.

title: Statistical Intuition Atrophy
en_def: The observed decline in a data professional's ability to recognize patterns, distributions, or anomalies independently of algorithmic assistance, following extended use of automated analysis systems.

title: Interpretability Theater
en_def: The presentation of model behavior explanations derived from automated interpretation frameworks where the explanations reflect the framework's approximation rather than the model's actual decision boundaries.

title: Performance-Value Gap
en_def: The divergence between a model's quantitative performance metrics and the actual business value generated, often obscured by the statistical focus of automated evaluation systems.

title: Metric Goodness Hallucination
en_def: The observation that practitioners attribute positive meaning to selected performance metrics without interrogating whether those metrics measure what the business context actually values.

title: Data Correlation Mythology
en_def: The pattern where discovered statistical associations between variables are interpreted as causal relationships due to presentation framing in algorithmic analysis outputs.

title: Dimensionality Reduction Opacity
en_def: The state where compressed data representations produced by algorithms lack transparent mapping to their original features, obscuring which information is preserved or lost.

title: Validation Set Leakage Blindness
en_def: The failure to detect information transfer from training data into validation processes through automated pipeline construction, resulting in optimistic performance estimates.

title: AI Selection Bias Blindness
en_def: The condition where automated variable selection introduces systematic biases that reflect the training data's demographic composition rather than genuine predictive relationships.

title: Confidence Score Mystification
en_def: The interpretation of numerical confidence outputs from models as direct measures of prediction accuracy, ignoring the model's calibration properties.

title: Anomaly Detection Confirmation
en_def: The tendency to interpret algorithm-identified outliers as true anomalies without domain verification, leading to either over-flagging or under-investigation of actual data quality issues.

title: Model Drift Invisibility
en_def: The phenomenon where changes in data distribution over time escape detection because monitoring occurs only at algorithmic performance points rather than continuous data behavior inspection.

title: Temporal Validation Illusion
en_def: The false assurance derived from out-of-sample testing where temporal ordering of events is not preserved, allowing information leakage from future time periods into past training phases.

title: Class Imbalance Invisibility
en_def: The overlooking of skewed outcome distributions where aggregate performance metrics mask poor performance on minority classes, particularly common in automated model evaluation.

title: Feature Importance Fixation
en_def: The reliance on algorithmic feature importance rankings as definitive measures of variable influence, without testing their stability across model specifications or data samples.

title: Hyperparameter Tuning Futility
en_def: Extensive optimization of model parameters within automated search spaces that can mask structural misspecification in the underlying model architecture.

title: Cross-Validation Rigidity
en_def: The mechanical application of cross-validation procedures that, while statistically sound, fail to accommodate domain-specific data dependencies or temporal structures.

title: Exploratory Data Atrophy
en_def: The reduction in manual data exploration activities as practitioners transition responsibility to automated data profiling and summarization systems.

title: Visualization Bias Acceptance
en_def: The phenomenon where visual presentations of data relationships chosen by algorithms become accepted as representative without interrogating the alternative visualizations not displayed.

title: Outlier Purging Regret
en_def: The recognition that automatically removed data points later proved relevant to understanding actual system behavior or revealed critical information about data generation conditions.

title: Missing Data Assumption Creep
en_def: The implicit assumptions about missingness patterns embedded in automated imputation algorithms, which often fail to align with the actual mechanisms generating missing values.

title: Normalization Mythology
en_def: The uncritical application of data normalization techniques based on the algorithm selected rather than examination of whether the transformation aligns with the data's underlying structure.

title: Sampling Strategy Invisibility
en_def: The use of algorithmic sampling approaches whose parameters and trade-offs remain opaque to practitioners implementing or interpreting their results.

title: Aggregation Distortion Blindness
en_def: The failure to recognize how information aggregation to particular granular levels produces summary statistics that misrepresent underlying variability or sub-group differences.

title: Benchmark Goodness Illusion
en_def: The interpretation of performance comparison against standard datasets as evidence of model suitability for different problem domains, despite differences in data characteristics.

title: Replication Crisis Unawareness
en_def: The presentation of findings from single model runs or data splits as generalizable results without acknowledging statistical variability across different random seeds or data partitions.

title: Algorithm Selection Cargo Cult
en_def: The practice of selecting data science algorithms based on apparent effectiveness in similar contexts without understanding the mechanisms that produce that effectiveness.

title: Default Parameter Dependency
en_def: The reliance on initial parameter settings of algorithms as reasonable starting points without systematic exploration of sensitivity to these choices.

title: Ensemble Opacity
en_def: The combining of multiple models into ensemble systems where the interaction effects between individual model predictions remain unexplored.

title: Data Provenance Amnesia
en_def: The loss of information about data origins, collection methods, and transformations as datasets pass through multiple processing pipelines.

title: Pipeline Automation Bottleneck
en_def: The emergence of complete dependence on automated pipeline systems such that manual workflow steps become conceptually unavailable when automation fails.

title: Metric Accumulation Paralysis
en_def: The state where the availability of numerous performance metrics creates decision ambiguity regarding which metrics warrant primary attention in model evaluation.

title: Ground Truth Inflation
en_def: The assumption that labeled training data accurately represents the phenomenon being modeled, without interrogation of labeling biases or measurement error in the labels themselves.

title: Transfer Learning Overconfidence
en_def: The expectation that models trained on related problems will perform well on new domains without empirical assessment of domain shift magnitude.

title: Regularization Vagueness
en_def: The application of regularization techniques whose strength and rationale remain unexpressed, leading to model behavior that cannot be explained or replicated.

title: Loss Function Arbitrariness
en_def: The selection of loss functions for optimization based on mathematical convenience rather than alignment with actual business outcomes or application requirements.

title: Gradient Descent Mystification
en_def: The handling of iterative optimization processes as black boxes whose convergence properties and local minima traps are not examined.

title: Activation Function Opacity
en_def: The use of nonlinear transformation functions in neural networks whose effects on information flow are not interrogated or tested.

title: Batch Normalization Dependency
en_def: The reliance on batch normalization layers where the statistical properties of batches are not examined for consistency with population characteristics.

title: Attention Mechanism Metaphor Slip
en_def: The interpretation of attention weights in neural networks as human-interpretable importance indicators despite their mathematical independence from perceptual attention.

title: Embedding Space Mythology
en_def: The assumption that geometric relationships in learned embedding spaces correspond to semantic or causal relationships in the original domain.

title: Clustering Silhouette Fixation
en_def: The reliance on internal validation metrics for clustering as definitive evidence of cluster quality without external domain verification.

title: Dimensionality Curse Invisibility
en_def: The overlooking of feature space expansion consequences where available samples become sparse relative to the number of variables being modeled.

title: Distance Metric Arbitrariness
en_def: The selection of distance or similarity measures for algorithms based on default availability rather than alignment with domain-specific relationships.

title: Probabilistic Calibration Ignorance
en_def: The interpretation of probability estimates as accurate likelihoods without testing the alignment between predicted probabilities and observed frequencies.

title: Threshold Optimization Myopia
en_def: The adjustment of decision thresholds to optimize training data performance without validation across different test populations or conditions.

title: Precision-Recall Tradeoff Opacity
en_def: The selection of performance thresholds without explicit articulation of the relative costs associated with false positives versus false negatives.

title: ROC Curve Theater
en_def: The presentation of AUC scores or ROC curves as model quality indicators without considering their limited applicability to imbalanced classification problems.

title: Confusion Matrix Neglect
en_def: The summarization of classification performance into single metrics without examination of the error distributions across different outcome categories.

title: Statistical Significance Drift
en_def: The interpretation of p-values or confidence intervals in high-dimensionality contexts where multiple comparisons and selection effects inflate false discovery rates.

title: Bayesian Posterior Confidence Collapse
en_def: The assumption that posterior distributions from Bayesian models represent genuine uncertainty about parameters despite potentially inappropriate prior specification.

title: Information Criterion Fetishism
en_def: The reliance on information criteria (AIC, BIC) for model selection as though they provide absolute quality judgments rather than relative comparisons.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Illusion
en_def: The attribution of universal validity to bootstrap confidence intervals despite their dependence on observed sample statistics and resampling assumptions.

title: Hypothesis Test Mechanicalism
en_def: The execution of statistical tests as procedural requirements without interrogation of their assumptions or interpretation of their results.

title: P-Value Misinterpretation Persistence
en_def: The interpretation of p-values as probabilities of hypotheses rather than the probability of observed data under null assumptions.

title: Effect Size Minimization
en_def: The emphasis on statistical significance while downplaying practical significance or effect magnitude in reporting analytic findings.

title: Causal Inference Confounding Blindness
en_def: The application of causal discovery algorithms without interrogation of unobserved confounders or the validity of assumptions underlying causal graph specification.

title: Instrumental Variable Assumption Stacking
en_def: The reliance on identified instrumental variables without empirical verification of the exclusion restriction or validity of the causal graph specification.

title: Regression Coefficient Determinism
en_def: The interpretation of point estimates of regression coefficients as definitive causal effects without uncertainty quantification or assumption checking.

title: Multicollinearity Invisibility
en_def: The oversight of high correlations among predictor variables that inflate parameter uncertainty without explicit diagnostics.

title: Heteroskedasticity Assumption Blindness
en_def: The application of models assuming constant error variance without verification of homoskedasticity assumptions.

title: Serial Correlation Oversight
en_def: The analysis of time series data with methods assuming independence of observations despite the presence of temporal autocorrelation.

title: Stationarity Assumption Invisibility
en_def: The application of time series models to non-stationary data without transformation or acknowledgment of violating fundamental method assumptions.

title: Forecasting Horizon Myopia
en_def: The evaluation of time series models on historical data without assessment of performance degradation as prediction horizons extend.

title: Survivorship Bias Invisibility
en_def: The analysis of data from entities that persist or succeed, while excluding entities that fail or disappear, producing biased statistical conclusions.

title: Selection Into Treatment Blindness
en_def: The comparison of outcomes between treated and untreated groups without addressing non-random assignment mechanisms.

title: Regression Discontinuity Assumption Slippage
en_def: The application of regression discontinuity designs without verification that discontinuities are sharp or that the running variable has not been altered.

title: Difference-in-Differences Parallel Trends Myth
en_def: The assumption that intervention and control groups would follow parallel outcome trajectories absent intervention, without empirical pre-intervention validation.

title: Matching Assumption Brittleness
en_def: The reliance on matched samples for causal inference without interrogation of the sensitivity of results to unobserved confounding.

title: Propensity Score Mythology
en_def: The handling of estimated propensity scores as definitive measures of selection probability without validation of their predictive accuracy.

title: Double Machine Learning Opacity
en_def: The application of double machine learning approaches where the debiasing mechanisms and their effectiveness remain unexplained.

title: Heterogeneous Intervention Effect Invisibility
en_def: The reporting of average intervention effects while obscuring the distribution of effects across population subgroups.

title: Policy Learning Overgeneralization
en_def: The derivation of general policy recommendations from estimated optimal policies trained on historical data with different environmental conditions.

title: Contextual Bandit Assumption Slippage
en_def: The application of contextual bandit algorithms where the Markov assumption or reward independence assumptions are violated.

title: Online Learning Concept Drift Ignorance
en_def: The deployment of online learning systems without monitoring for changes in the underlying data generating process.

title: Active Learning Budget Myopia
en_def: The selection of samples for labeling through active learning without consideration of the opportunity costs of annotation resources.

title: Weak Supervision Label Noise Blindness
en_def: The incorporation of weak labels from automated or crowdsourced sources without explicit modeling of label quality or noise rates.

title: Data Augmentation Assumption Creep
en_def: The application of data augmentation techniques that implicitly assume invariance properties that may not hold in the actual application domain.

title: Synthetic Data Fidelity Overestimation
en_def: The assumption that synthetic data generated through simulation or neural networks possesses distributional characteristics matching real data.

title: Federated Learning Privacy Theater
en_def: The presentation of federated learning architectures as inherently privacy-preserving without examining the possibility of model inversion or membership inference.

title: Differential Privacy Budget Opacity
en_def: The application of differential privacy mechanisms where the privacy loss and utility trade-offs remain unexplained.

title: Fairness Metric Circularity
en_def: The selection of fairness criteria that, by design, validate the algorithmic choices previously embedded in the model.

title: Algorithmic Debiasing False Confidence
en_def: The application of debiasing algorithms as though they eliminate unfairness without validation that the fairness criteria themselves are appropriately specified.

title: Representation Gap Invisibility
en_def: The oversight of subgroup performance disparities in model output despite aggregate fairness metrics indicating sufficient parity.

title: Fairness-Accuracy Tradeoff Dismissal
en_def: The assertion that fairness constraints do not degrade overall model performance without empirical assessment of performance across group definitions.

title: Causal Fairness Assumption Brittleness
en_def: The reliance on causal fairness criteria where the causal graph specification incorporates normative judgments presented as technical decisions.

title: Counterfactual Fairness Circularity
en_def: The definition of fairness through counterfactual scenarios where the specification of what-if conditions embeds policy preferences.

title: Model Card Compliance Theater
en_def: The production of documentation describing model properties as though complete disclosure was achieved without substantive interrogation of model limitations.

title: Transparency Maximization Paradox
en_def: The expansion of model documentation and disclosure until the information volume exceeds the cognitive capacity of its intended audience.

title: Responsible AI Ambiguity
en_def: The application of 'responsible AI' frameworks where the criteria for responsibility remain undefined or conflict across implementation contexts.

title: Governance Theater Proliferation
en_def: The establishment of institutional review processes for model deployment that create compliance appearance without substantive impact reduction.

title: External Validation Abdication
en_def: The reliance on model developers to conduct validation studies without independent external scrutiny of claims or methodology.

title: Regulatory Arbitrage Exploitation
en_def: The deployment of models in jurisdictions or contexts where regulatory oversight remains nascent or inconsistently applied.

title: Data Science Professionalism Void
en_def: The absence of established professional standards, certification bodies, and disciplinary mechanisms comparable to traditional engineering disciplines.
